discoverer: Ernest Rutherford & Robert Owens / Friedrich Ernst Dorn (independently)
etymology: From radium — radon was first identified as a gaseous product ('emanation') released by radium during its radioactive decay. The name radon reflects this origin: radium + the noble gas suffix -on (following neon, argon, krypton, xenon). Ramsay and Gray formally proposed 'radon' in 1923 after confirming it was a true noble gas.

timeline.event: Ernest Rutherford and Robert Owens, at McGill University in Montreal, observe that thorium compounds release a radioactive gas — they call it 'thorium emanation.' Rutherford demonstrates it has a definite half-life, making this one of the first observations of radioactive decay kinetics.

Friedrich Ernst Dorn, a German physicist at Halle, discovers that radium also releases a radioactive gas — 'radium emanation.' This is now known to be radon-222. The observation is made independently of Rutherford's thorium emanation work.

Rutherford and Frederick Soddy demonstrate that radioactive decay involves the transmutation of one element into another — using thorium emanation as one of their key examples. This revolutionary conclusion overturns the Victorian belief that atoms are immutable and earns Rutherford the 1908 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

William Ramsay and Robert Whytlaw-Gray collect enough radium emanation to measure its density and atomic weight — confirming it is a heavy noble gas fitting between xenon and the predicted end of Period 6 in the periodic table. They temporarily name it 'niton' from the Latin nitens (shining) for its luminescence.
The name 'radon' is officially adopted — from radium, its parent element in the decay chain. The name replaces both 'radium emanation' and 'niton.'

The first evidence linking radon to lung cancer emerges from studies of uranium miners in Schneeberg and Joachimsthal (now in Germany and Czech Republic) — miners in these areas had been dying of lung disease for centuries, and the cause is now identified as radon accumulation in the mines.

Stanley Watras, a nuclear plant construction worker in Pennsylvania, repeatedly triggers radiation monitors entering work — investigations reveal his home has radon levels 700 times the EPA action level. The discovery launches widespread public awareness of indoor radon as a health hazard, and radon testing of homes becomes standard in the US and Europe.

story: Radon was discovered not as a substance but as a behavior — the mysterious radioactive 'breath' exhaled by thorium and radium. Rutherford's observation that this emanation had a definite half-life was one of the first demonstrations that radioactive decay follows predictable exponential kinetics, a discovery that transformed physics. Radon itself is colorless, odorless, and tasteless — completely undetectable without instruments — which makes it one of the more insidious environmental hazards. It seeps from soil and rock into basements and ground-floor rooms, where it accumulates if not ventilated. In uranium-mining regions of Europe, miners had been dying of what was called 'mountain sickness' for 400 years before radon was identified as the cause. The element discovered as a ghost — invisible decay product of an invisible element — continues to kill more people annually than any other form of radiation exposure.
source_note: Thorium emanation observed by Rutherford and Owens (1899). Radium emanation by Dorn (1900). Density measured by Ramsay and Whytlaw-Gray (1908). Named radon (1923).
